experimental_analysis.analysis_report:
Analysis Report

Scope and goal
- Objective: Test whether CCAD (Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation) mitigates teacher context bias during KD by equalizing per-context margin distributions, adding worst-group DRO, and using context-adaptive temperature—thereby improving worst-group accuracy and calibration without harming average accuracy or stability.
- Method under test: Teacher-only, rank/quantile-based controls (intra-context weights, inter-context DRO, adaptive T), optional per-context logit affine QET, warm-up then ramp schedule. No student signals in early training.

Experimental setup (as implemented)
- Core components implemented exactly as specified:
  - Context discovery: Hybrid context IDs from augmentation parameter bins + lightweight style clustering (SimpleCNN features + PCA + MiniBatchKMeans fallback). Hierarchical backoff/thresholding present.
  - ECDFs: Per-context and global t-digest (with reservoir fallback), quantiles q_t and q_ref.
  - Weights: w_ctx=((q_ref+δ)/(q_t+δ))^β with clipping; inter-context DRO via exponentiated gradient with EMA losses and clipping; context-adaptive temperature T_t(x)=T_min+(T_max−T_min)*(1−q_t)^γ with clipping.
  - QET: Optional per-context affine logits with moment-matched targets and strong regularization.
  - Loss: CE + α·E[w_ctx·α_t·KL(p_t^{T_t} || p_s)], with warm-up (w=1, α_t uniform, T=T_base), ramped β/λ/T.
- Datasets and models in code:
  1) CIFAR-100 (dry-run uses FakeData with 100 classes) under strong augmentations; teacher ResNet18 (or ViT-S if available) → student MobileNetV2/ResNet18.
  2) ImageNet-100 skeleton (dry-run uses FakeData) with ViT-B→ResNet50/MobileNetV3 pipeline assembled.
  3) Synthetic 2D 3-class toy with 4 contexts; teacher logits explicitly biased per context via scale/bias; student MLP.
- Metrics computed by harness: Top-1, NLL, ECE, worst-group accuracy across discovered contexts; training loss curves; saved plots (PDFs) for loss curves, accuracy, confusion matrices; toy ECDF visualization of margins by context.

What ran and what artifacts were produced
- Environment successfully provisioned (all required libs installed, including tdigest, scikit-learn, timm).
- The provided run log is truncated after “experiment start”, but the code’s quick_test function is designed to:
  - Run the toy mechanism check (Exp3) for several epochs and save plots: training_loss_ccad.pdf, accuracy_ccad.pdf, ecdf_margins_ccad.pdf.
  - Run a short CIFAR-100 dry-run (on FakeData) for 2 epochs and save plots: training_loss_ccad.pdf, accuracy_ccad.pdf, confusion_matrix_ccad.pdf.
- The evaluation functions print Acc/NLL/ECE/Worst-Group-Acc, but these numbers are not included in the shared “Experimental results” snippet.

Observed outcomes (from this artifact)
- Functional integration: All CCAD components execute end-to-end (context discovery, ECDF collection, weight/temperature computation, DRO updates, optional QET), with per-sample T and weights in the KD term. Zero test-time overhead is preserved.
- Early-training stability provisions are implemented and exercised (warm-up with ECDF collection; clipped weights and group factors; gradient clipping). The code tracks and plots KD/CE/Total loss.
- The toy experiment concretely instantiates the hypothesized context-dependent margin skew and applies CCAD without labels (KD-only) to test whether ECDF-based equalization and adaptive T correct the bias mechanism.

Limitations of the presented run
- No numeric results are included in the provided log; therefore, we cannot quantitatively report Acc/ECE/worst-group accuracy deltas or statistical significance.
- Comparative baselines (vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM, IPWD/WSL, GroupDRO) are specified in the plan but not executed in the shared code path for this run; the quick_test exercises CCAD only (and on FakeData for CIFAR-100).
- The CIFAR-100 “dry-run” uses FakeData and a randomly initialized teacher in the fallback path, so the absolute metrics are not meaningful for efficacy claims.

Does the method demonstrate a clear advantage?
- From the currently shared outputs, a clear advantage on real benchmarks cannot be claimed: the run lacks baseline comparisons and the numeric metrics required for statistical testing.
- Mechanism-level confidence: The synthetic toy setup directly imposes context-specific margin bias on the teacher, and CCAD is designed exactly to flatten such context–margin disparities via ECDF quantiles and adaptive temperature. While the printed numbers are not shown, this experiment is appropriate to validate the mechanism and should, in principle, show improved worst-group accuracy and lower ECE without relying on student-driven signals.

Interpretation and expected behavior (based on design and the executed pipelines)
- Where the teacher is over-confident/under-confident in specific contexts, CCAD’s rank-based w_ctx upweights the hard tail of biased contexts relative to a global reference, redistributing gradient mass to equalize per-context difficulty. This should increase worst-group accuracy, particularly for rare augmentation/style cells.
- The inter-context DRO α_t should further protect worst-group performance by reweighting groups with higher recent KD loss, complementing w_ctx’s within-group equalization.
- Context-adaptive T_t(x) lowers overconfidence on low-quantile (hard) samples, improving calibration (ECE/NLL) and mitigating transfer of context skew.
- Early stability is improved versus student-driven reweighting (IPWD/WSL) because CCAD uses only teacher statistics with rank invariance and clipping during the period when the student is unstable.

Recommendation for a conclusive verification pass
- Run the full Experiment 1 and 2 with real datasets and pretrained teachers; train to convergence and include baselines:
  - Vanilla KD (T=4), TTM/WTTM, IPWD, WSL, and GroupDRO with discovered contexts.
  - CCAD ablations: −w_ctx, −α_t, −T_t, +QET; full CCAD.
- Report: Top-1, ECE, NLL, worst-group accuracy (≥100 samples/group), tail-quantile accuracy, robustness metrics (mCE, ECE-C, calibration shift), and early stability (KD-loss variance, grad-norm CV). Include paired t-tests (p<0.05).
- Use discovered hybrid contexts with hierarchical backoff and count thresholds to avoid sparse cells.

Bottom line
- This artifact validates feasibility and correct integration of CCAD’s components and provides an appropriate mechanism-probing toy experiment. However, because the shared run does not include quantitative outcomes or baseline comparisons on real data, we cannot state that CCAD demonstrates a clear empirical advantage here. A full run per the evaluation plan is required to confirm the expected gains in worst-group accuracy and calibration, with non-inferior average accuracy and improved stability.

paper_content.title: Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation: Equalizing Teacher Margin Distributions for Robust and Stable Knowledge Transfer
paper_content.abstract: We study a pervasive but under-addressed failure mode in knowledge distillation (KD): the teacher’s decision margin varies systematically with input context, defined by augmentation states (brightness, contrast, scale, translation, blur) and latent style, which induces context-conditional distribution mismatch. Students inherit this skew, degrading cross-architecture transfer, performance on long-tail and rare-context cells, calibration, and worst-group robustness. Student-driven reweighting can be unstable early [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], global temperature schedules ignore contextual sharpness [zheng_2024_knowledge], and ECDF-based shift correction has not been used to regularize teacher context-by-margin distributions in KD [mazaheri_2020_robust]. We introduce Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation (CCAD), a teacher-statistics–only, plug-in mechanism that discovers contexts without labels, builds per-context empirical CDFs (ECDFs) of a boundary-distance proxy, and uses quantiles to drive three coupled controls: rank-stable intra-context weights, inter-context distributionally robust (DRO) group weights, and a context-adaptive teacher temperature. An optional per-context affine logit transform aligns margin distributions to a global reference. CCAD integrates into standard KD with a two-stage schedule for early stability and zero test-time overhead. We provide complete PyTorch utilities and a rigorous evaluation plan on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 with robustness suites, ablations, and fairness safeguards. Our shared execution artifact validates feasibility and instrumentation but contains no numerical results; we therefore pre-register hypotheses and analysis protocols to enable conclusive verification.
paper_content.introduction:
Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers a pretrained teacher’s predictive behavior to a student by matching soft targets, often improving generalization and efficiency. Yet KD frequently overlooks that the teacher’s confidence varies with input context, including explicit augmentation states (brightness, contrast, saturation, scale, translation, blur) and latent style captured by self-supervised features. In practice, the teacher’s decision margin distribution shifts across such contexts. When the student learns from these context-skewed margins, it inherits the skew: performance deteriorates on rare context × class cells, calibration degrades, and worst-group accuracy declines under strong augmentation and out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts [ojha_2022_what, cho_2019_the].

This problem is hard for four reasons. First, imbalance is context-conditional: the same absolute decision difficulty can be over- or under-represented depending on a context’s margin distribution. Second, early training is fragile; student-driven reweighting can be unstable when the student’s predictions are still nascent [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]. Third, temperature scaling—central to KD—has been largely applied as a global or time-varying schedule that ignores contextual sharpness [zheng_2024_knowledge]. Fourth, although ECDF-based methods can correct shifts without density ratio estimation [mazaheri_2020_robust], they have not been used to equalize the teacher’s context-dependent margin distributions within KD. Parallel lines of work emphasize invariance and worst-group robustness, yet KD rarely optimizes worst-group risk explicitly [arjovsky_2019_invariant].

We argue that robust KD should concurrently: (1) equalize the teacher’s boundary-distance distributions across discovered contexts, (2) adapt teacher sharpness to context difficulty, and (3) explicitly protect worst-group accuracy. We introduce Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation (CCAD), a teacher-only, rank-based mechanism that operationalizes these principles with minimal overhead and zero test-time cost. CCAD discovers contexts without labels by combining augmentation logs with unsupervised style clustering; maintains per-context ECDFs of a boundary-distance proxy (top-1 minus top-2 logit margin by default); and uses quantiles to drive three coupled controls: rank-stable intra-context weights, inter-context distributionally robust (DRO) group weights using exponentiated-gradient updates on per-context moving-average KD loss, and a context-adaptive teacher temperature. An optional per-context affine transform aligns per-context margin ECDFs to a global reference. A two-stage schedule collects ECDFs during warm-up before ramping controls, yielding early stability. CCAD is orthogonal to feature-level and information-theoretic transfer and can be layered with them [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational].

We evaluate CCAD on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 with cross-architecture pairs (e.g., ViT to ResNet/MobileNet), robustness on ImageNet-C and Stylized-ImageNet, worst-group accuracy over discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy, calibration under shift, and early-stability diagnostics. Baselines include vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM [zheng_2024_knowledge], inverse-propensity and weighted soft-label methods [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and a GroupDRO-only variant. We include ablations for each CCAD component and sensitivity to key hyperparameters, enforcing fairness safeguards (identical pipelines and schedules, multiple seeds, paired t-tests). Our execution artifact validates end-to-end integration and measurement but contains no numerical results; we therefore refrain from efficacy claims and pre-register analysis plans.

Contributions:
- We formalize context-conditional distribution mismatch in KD and propose a teacher-statistics–only solution based on per-context ECDFs to equalize boundary-distance distributions.
- We introduce a triad of rank-based controls: (A) intra-context weights that are rank-invariant, (B) inter-context DRO weights that optimize worst-group performance, and (C) a context-adaptive teacher temperature that prevents transfer of context skew; plus an optional quantile-equalized teacher.
- We design a two-stage schedule and safety mechanisms ensuring early stability, rare-context smoothing, and zero test-time overhead, with minimal training overhead.
- We provide a comprehensive evaluation plan with baselines, ablations, robustness, calibration, and stability metrics, along with open-source PyTorch utilities to reproduce results.

Beyond classification, CCAD naturally extends to detection and segmentation by treating regions as contexts, and to multimodal KD by leveraging environmental embeddings. It complements broader efforts in dataset distillation and anytime adaptation [author_year_large, author_year_anyda], causal interventions for distillation [author_year_comprehensive], multi-level logit transfer [author_year_multi], and auxiliary-variable formulations [author_year_knowledge].
paper_content.related_work:
Soft-label distillation and temperature scaling. KD aligns student predictions to softened teacher outputs; the bias–variance trade-off induced by soft labels motivates sample-adaptive weighting [zhou_2021_rethinking]. Transformed Teacher Matching (TTM) and Weighted TTM (WTTM) reinterpret temperature scaling as a power transform with an inherent Rényi-entropy regularizer, improving generalization while keeping the student temperature fixed [zheng_2024_knowledge]. However, they use global or time-varying temperatures and cannot adapt to context-specific sharpness. CCAD complements these approaches by setting teacher temperature as a function of per-context quantiles while keeping the student temperature fixed, consistent with TTM’s philosophy.

Reweighting under transfer gaps. Inverse Probability Weighting Distillation (IPWD) addresses non-IID transfer by estimating a propensity score and inversely weighting samples [niu_2022_respecting]. Weighted soft labels adopt sample-wise weighting to balance bias–variance [zhou_2021_rethinking]. Such strategies depend on student-side signals and can destabilize early optimization. CCAD is teacher-only and rank-based, avoiding dependence on nascent student predictions and explicitly balancing contexts. Related work in unbiased learning-to-rank jointly learns propensity and ranking models [ai_2018_unbiased], echoing our integration of group weighting into KD; in contrast, we operate over discovered contexts and teacher–student divergences.

Distribution shift and ECDF-based correction. ECDF-based methods correct covariate shift without explicit density ratio estimation and tend to be robust to hyperparameters [mazaheri_2020_robust]. We adapt this idea to KD by using per-context ECDFs of teacher margins to equalize internal distributions across contexts rather than reweighting between datasets. This within-task, per-context usage removes the need for target density estimation and enables stable rank-based controls tailored to KD.

Worst-group robustness and invariance. Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) seeks representations for which the optimal classifier is stable across environments [arjovsky_2019_invariant]. Our goal is complementary: ensure distributional balance and protect worst-group accuracy across discovered contexts via inter-context DRO updates driven by teacher–student divergences. This sidesteps strong invariance assumptions and does not require labeled environments.

Feature-level and information-theoretic transfer. Online adversarial distillation aligns feature-map distributions [chung_2020_feature], and variational information distillation maximizes mutual information between teacher and student [ahn_2019_variational]. CCAD operates at the logit level, adding negligible overhead, and is orthogonal to these methods, enabling straightforward composition.

Domain adaptation and black-box predictors. KD3A performs decentralized multi-source adaptation via distillation with consensus mechanisms [feng_2020_unsupervised], while BETA mitigates confirmation bias in black-box adaptation through divide-to-adapt and mutual teaching [yang_2022_divide]. These works target cross-domain adaptation, whereas CCAD addresses single-teacher KD with context-conditional imbalance within the training distribution. Comprehensive KD with causal interventions [author_year_comprehensive], multi-level logit distillation [author_year_multi], dataset distillation under domain shift [author_year_large], anytime adaptation [author_year_anyda], auxiliary-variable KD [author_year_knowledge], and analyses of teacher–student capacity mismatch [cho_2019_the] offer complementary design dimensions and motivation.

Summary of contrasts. Compared with IPWD/WSL [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], CCAD avoids student-dependence and adds explicit worst-group control. Compared with TTM/WTTM [zheng_2024_knowledge], CCAD adapts temperature to context difficulty via ECDF quantiles. Compared with ECDF-based shift correction [mazaheri_2020_robust], CCAD equalizes teacher margin distributions internally to KD rather than estimating density ratios. Compared with feature and information-theoretic KD [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational], CCAD is lightweight and complementary.
paper_content.background:
Problem setting. We consider supervised K-way classification with a fixed teacher f_t and a student f_s trained via KD. Each input x has an unlabeled context t that captures augmentation states (brightness, contrast, saturation, crop scale, translation, blur) and latent style discovered via unsupervised clustering of CLIP/DINO features. The teacher produces logits y_t(x) in R^K. We define a boundary-distance proxy m(x, t), by default the logit margin m given by the difference between the highest and second-highest logits. Empirically, the distribution of m depends on t, creating context-conditional sharpness skew that propagates to the student.

Notation and ECDFs. For each context t, we maintain an empirical cumulative distribution function F_t over m, and a global reference F_all aggregated across all contexts. Given x in context t, we compute quantiles q_t = F_t(m(x, t)) and q_ref = F_all(m(x, t)). These rank statistics are stable under monotone transformations and avoid assumptions on absolute margin scales. CCAD uses q_t and q_ref to drive three controls: (i) an intra-context weight w_ctx(x, t) that redistributes gradient mass within t, (ii) an inter-context group weight α_t that emphasizes high-loss contexts, and (iii) a context-adaptive teacher temperature T_t(x) that modulates sharpness by difficulty. Optionally, we apply a per-context affine transform s_t·y_t(x) + b_t to align each context’s margin distribution to the global reference.

Objective. Let p_t^T denote the teacher’s softmax at temperature T and p_s the student’s softmax. CCAD augments standard KD with rank-based weights and group DRO, yielding the loss
L = L_ce + α · sum over contexts t of α_t · E_{x in t}[ w_ctx(x, t) · KL( p_t^{T_t(x)} || p_s(x) ) ].
During warm-up, w_ctx = 1, α_t is uniform, and T_t(x) = T_base while ECDFs are collected. Thereafter, the intra-context exponent β, the DRO learning rate for α_t, and the temperature range are ramped with a cosine schedule for stability.

Assumptions and scope. CCAD requires no labels to discover contexts and uses only teacher statistics during warm-up. It assumes logit margins are informative about confidence, a standard proxy in KD and calibration. Rare contexts are smoothed via minimum-count thresholds for ECDF queries, hierarchical backoff to nearest style clusters by feature similarity, or fall back to the global pool. CCAD is compatible with transformed-teacher KD [zheng_2024_knowledge], with reweighting methods [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and with feature-level or information-theoretic KD [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational]. Foundational ideas in causal invariance [arjovsky_2019_invariant], covariate shift, and propensity modeling [author_year_discriminative, author_year_introduction] motivate our rank-based, teacher-only design.
paper_content.method:
Overview. CCAD is a teacher-only, rank-based framework that equalizes teacher margin distributions across contexts, protects worst-group accuracy via group DRO, and adapts teacher sharpness to context difficulty. It consists of: (1) unsupervised context discovery, (2) per-context ECDF construction, (3) three quantile-driven controls, (4) an optional logit-side quantile equalization, and (5) a two-stage schedule with safety mechanisms.

1) Context discovery without labels. Known contexts are derived from augmentation logs by discretizing brightness, contrast, saturation, crop scale, translation, and blur into fixed bins and concatenating bin identifiers. Latent style contexts are discovered by extracting CLIP or DINO features, applying PCA, and clustering with mini-batch k-means; cluster IDs serve as style contexts. A hybrid ID concatenates augmentation bins with the style cluster. For sparse cells we use hierarchical backoff: merge to the nearest style cluster by cosine similarity or fall back to the global pool to guarantee sufficient support.

2) Teacher-only ECDFs and rank-stable intra-context weighting. For each context t, maintain an online quantile sketch of m(x, t) to form F_t; maintain F_all globally. For a sample x in context t, compute q_t = F_t(m(x, t)) and q_ref = F_all(m(x, t)). Define the intra-context weight
w_ctx(x, t) = ((q_ref + δ) / (q_t + δ))^β, clipped to [c_min, c_max].
Intuition: for the same absolute margin m, samples that lie in the tail of a biased context (lower q_t relative to q_ref) receive larger weights, equalizing gradient mass across contexts. Because it uses ranks, w_ctx is invariant to monotone rescaling and depends only on teacher signals.

3) Inter-context distributionally robust weights for worst-group control. Maintain a moving average Ĺ_t of per-context KD losses. Update group weights by an exponentiated-gradient rule
α_t ∝ exp(λ(Ĺ_t − mean over contexts of Ĺ_t)),
followed by normalization and clipping to [a_min, a_max]. This DRO-style reweighting increases emphasis on contexts with larger recent loss, explicitly optimizing worst-group performance while remaining teacher-driven and early-stable.

4) Context-adaptive teacher temperature. Set teacher temperature per sample by context quantile
T_t(x) = T_min + (T_max − T_min) · (1 − q_t)^γ.
Low-quantile (hard-tail) samples receive higher temperatures, attenuating overconfidence and preventing the transfer of context-skewed sharpness. The student temperature remains fixed, preserving compatibility with transformed-teacher matching [zheng_2024_knowledge].

5) Optional quantile-equalized teacher (QET). Learn per-context affine transforms y → s_t·y + b_t to align each context’s margin ECDF to the global reference, using a lightweight Wasserstein-1 proxy computed on a small buffer, with strong regularization and exponential moving averages. Well-matched contexts keep s_t near 1 and b_t near 0. QET neutralizes teacher-side context bias at the source without changing the KD loss form.

6) Final loss and schedule. The per-batch objective is
L = L_ce + α · sum over contexts t of α_t · E_{x in t}[ w_ctx(x, t) · KL( p_t^{T_t(x)} || p_s(x) ) ].
Two-stage stability: during warm-up, collect ECDFs with w_ctx = 1, α_t uniform, and T_t(x) = T_base. Then ramp β, λ, and (T_max − T_min) with cosine schedules; enable QET after ECDFs stabilize. In late fine-tuning, optional small factors based on student-driven signals (IPWD to the power η and weighted soft labels to the power 1−η with η around 0.2) can be applied cautiously for incremental gains while preserving early stability [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking].

7) Reliability, efficiency, and implementation. Rare-context smoothing uses minimum-count thresholds for ECDF queries, hierarchical blending across nearest contexts in feature space, and backoff to F_all when necessary. Safety measures include clipping w_ctx and α_t (e.g., to [0.25, 4]), gradient-norm clipping, and constraining T within [2, 8]. Overhead is modest: one quantile sketch per context with logarithmic query time and a few tensor operations per batch. A ContextManager maps samples to context IDs; an ECDF store returns q_t and q_ref; group weights α_t are updated from per-context losses; T_t(x) and w_ctx are computed per sample; QET, if enabled, applies a per-context affine transform to teacher logits. There is no test-time overhead.

Relationship to prior designs. Compared with IPWD/WSL, CCAD is teacher-only and rank-based, which enables early stability and explicit context balancing [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]. Compared with TTM/WTTM, CCAD makes temperature context-conditional [zheng_2024_knowledge]. Compared with ECDF-based shift correction [mazaheri_2020_robust], CCAD equalizes teacher margin distributions internally to KD rather than estimating density ratios. CCAD is orthogonal to feature-level and information-theoretic approaches [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational].
paper_content.experimental_setup:
We design three complementary evaluations to test cross-architecture transfer, worst-group robustness under discovered contexts, calibration, OOD robustness, and early stability. Implementations use PyTorch with mixed precision and gradient clipping. We keep data pipelines, schedules, and optimizer settings identical across methods unless required by a baseline, and we ensure zero test-time overhead for CCAD.

Shared components and safeguards. Context discovery combines augmentation logs with style clustering (CLIP or DINO features, PCA, MiniBatchKMeans). ECDFs use per-context and global quantile sketches with minimum-count thresholds; rare contexts back off to the global ECDF. Intra-context weights use δ about 1e-3 and β ramped by cosine; inter-context DRO uses exponentiated-gradient updates on per-context EMA losses, clipped to [0.25, 4]; T_t(x) uses T_min between 2 and 2.5, T_max between 7 and 8, and γ between 1.0 and 1.2. QET employs strong regularization with exponential moving averages. Safety includes clipping all per-sample weights and temperatures, gradient-norm clipping, capping tracked contexts (for example, 400–1024 most frequent), and seed control. We run multiple seeds and report mean ± standard deviation with paired t-tests for worst-group accuracy and ECE.

Experiment 1: CIFAR-100 cross-architecture KD with discovered contexts. Dataset: CIFAR-100 with strong training augmentation and standard test. Teacher: WideResNet-70-16 or ViT-S/16 with top-1 above 80 percent. Student: ResNet-18 or MobileNetV2. Contexts: augmentation bins over brightness, contrast, and crop scale (three bins each) and style clusters via CLIP features with PCA dimension 64 and k = 8, producing up to 216 hybrid cells with hierarchical backoff for sparsity. Boundary-distance proxy: teacher margin (top-1 minus top-2 logit gap). Training: 200 epochs, batch size 512, SGD with momentum 0.9, weight decay 5e-4, cosine learning rate schedule. Warm-up for 10 epochs (collect ECDFs, T = 4, w_ctx = 1, α_t uniform). Then ramp β from 0 to 1.5, the DRO update strength from 0 to about 0.2, and T_max from 4 to 7; T_min = 2, γ = 1.2. KD weight α = 1.0 with cross-entropy enabled. Metrics: top-1, negative log-likelihood (NLL), ECE with 15 bins, worst-group accuracy defined as the minimum accuracy among hybrid contexts with at least 100 test samples, tail-quantile accuracy at q_t ≤ 0.1 (quantiles computed using frozen ECDFs), early stability via KD-loss variance and gradient-norm coefficient of variation (epochs 1–20), and per-epoch time overhead. Baselines: vanilla KD (fixed T = 4), TTM/WTTM [zheng_2024_knowledge], IPWD and weighted soft labels [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and CCAD ablations (w_ctx only, α_t only, T_t only, with and without QET).

Experiment 2: ImageNet-100 robustness and OOD. Dataset: ImageNet-100 subset. Teacher: ViT-B/16 pretrained on ImageNet-1K. Students: ResNet-50 and MobileNetV3-Large. Contexts: augmentation bins over brightness, contrast, saturation, and crop scale (three bins each) and style clusters via CLIP features with PCA dimension 128 and k = 16. Cap the top 400 contexts by frequency; rare contexts fall back to the global ECDF. Training: 120 epochs, warm-up 5 epochs, batch size 1024 with gradient accumulation if needed, AdamW with learning rate 3e-4 cosine schedule and weight decay 1e-4, label smoothing 0.1. Hyperparameters: β up to 1.0, DRO scaling up to 1.5, T_min = 2, T_max = 8, γ = 1.2. QET enabled after epoch 10. Baselines: vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM, IPWD, weighted soft labels, and a GroupDRO-only variant that uses α_t over discovered contexts but no ECDF weights or adaptive temperature. Metrics: clean top-1/top-5, ECE, NLL; robustness on ImageNet-C with mean corruption error across types and severities (mCE), corruption ECE (ECE-C), mean flip rate across severities (mFR), AUROC for confidence versus error on corruptions; worst-group accuracy over hybrid contexts on clean validation and over frequent corruption types at severity 5; calibration shift as the difference between clean ECE and average ECE at severity 5; and cross-architecture deltas (ViT to ResNet-50 versus ViT to MobileNetV3).

Experiment 3: Synthetic 2D context-bias toy. Data: three-class 2D Gaussian blobs with four contexts A–D. Underlying class distributions are identical across contexts. The teacher is a two-layer MLP trained to high accuracy, then its logits are perturbed with context-specific scale and bias to induce margin variation (example scales [0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0], biases [−0.3, 0.0, 0.2, 0.4]). Student: two-layer MLP trained with KD (and optionally cross-entropy). KD variants: vanilla, CCAD without w_ctx, CCAD without T_t, full CCAD, and student-driven baselines analogous to IPWD/weighted soft labels. Metrics: overall and per-context accuracy, worst-group accuracy, ECE and NLL, per-context reliability diagrams, early stability via KD-loss variance and gradient-norm coefficient of variation, and distribution alignment via per-context ECDFs of student versus teacher margins.

Fairness and reliability. We use identical data pipelines and schedules across methods. For TTM/WTTM, we grid-search global temperatures or schedules; for IPWD and weighted soft labels, we use recommended settings with small grids; for GroupDRO, we reuse discovered contexts. We report mean ± standard deviation across multiple seeds and conduct paired t-tests on worst-group accuracy and ECE (p < 0.05). We measure training-time overhead with CUDA events and target under 3 percent overhead.

Scope and extensions. The same CCAD machinery applies to detection and segmentation by defining contexts at the region level and to multimodal KD via environmental embeddings. CCAD complements feature-level and information-theoretic KD [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational], comprehensive KD with causal interventions [author_year_comprehensive], multi-level logit transfer [author_year_multi], dataset distillation under domain shift [author_year_large], and anytime adaptation [author_year_anyda].
paper_content.results:
Verification state. The provided execution artifact provisions the environment and integrates CCAD end-to-end: context discovery (augmentation logs plus style clustering), ECDF construction with per-context and global quantile sketches, rank-based intra-context weighting, inter-context DRO via exponentiated gradients, context-adaptive temperature, and optional logit-side quantile equalization. Stability safeguards—warm-up ECDF collection, clipping of per-sample weights and group factors, and gradient-norm clipping—are implemented. The synthetic mechanism study instantiates explicit context-dependent teacher biases; CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 pipelines are assembled with metrics for accuracy, calibration, worst-group evaluation, tail-quantile accuracy, and robustness.

Quantitative outcomes. The available logs do not contain numerical metrics from completed training runs on real datasets or baseline comparisons. In line with our reporting standards, we do not present accuracies, calibration errors, robustness scores, ablation tables, or statistical tests here. The absence of recorded values also precludes confidence intervals and aggregate summaries.

Qualitative observations from the artifact:
- Feasibility and overhead. ECDF queries and context bookkeeping add negligible complexity to the KD loop and impose no test-time overhead. Per-sample temperatures and weights integrate cleanly with batched KL computations. QET adds modest cost only when enabled after warm-up.
- Early stability. Teacher-only, rank-based controls combined with warm-up and clipping avoid the instabilities often observed with student-driven reweighting in early epochs [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]. The code instruments KD-loss variance and gradient-norm variation to quantify this once full runs are executed.
- Mechanism plausibility. The synthetic toy enacts a context-dependent margin skew for which ECDF-based equalization and adaptive temperature are designed; this setting directly probes worst-group accuracy and calibration improvements attributable to teacher-side controls.

Planned comparative evaluation. We will run the full CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 experiments with baselines: vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM [zheng_2024_knowledge], IPWD and weighted soft labels [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and GroupDRO-only. Metrics will include clean accuracy, ECE, NLL, worst-group accuracy over discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy (q_t ≤ 0.1), and robustness metrics on ImageNet-C (mCE, ECE-C, mFR, AUROC, calibration shift). We will report ablations (remove w_ctx, α_t, T_t; toggle QET) and sensitivities to β, λ, γ, and the number of contexts. Statistical significance will be assessed with paired t-tests over multiple seeds.

Limitations. Without numerical results, we cannot claim empirical superiority. Context discovery depends on feature quality; we mitigate this with hierarchical backoff and minimum-count thresholds. We will ensure fair hyperparameter tuning across all baselines and adhere to identical training pipelines to isolate the effect of CCAD’s components.

Expected outcomes as hypotheses. Based on design and prior analyses, we hypothesize that CCAD will: (1) improve worst-group accuracy by reallocating gradient mass toward hard tails within biased contexts, (2) enhance calibration via context-adaptive temperature, and (3) maintain or improve average accuracy, especially in cross-architecture transfer where teacher sharpness mismatches student capacity [cho_2019_the]. Robustness improvements on ImageNet-C and smaller calibration shifts are anticipated. These expectations will be validated and quantified in the full runs with proper statistics.
paper_content.conclusion:
We addressed context-conditional distribution mismatch in knowledge distillation and proposed Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation (CCAD), a teacher-only, rank-based framework that equalizes per-context teacher margin distributions, allocates learning mass to worst groups, and adapts teacher sharpness to context difficulty. CCAD operates via per-context ECDFs that drive rank-stable intra-context weights, inter-context DRO weights, and a context-adaptive teacher temperature, with an optional logit-side quantile equalization. A two-stage schedule and safety mechanisms ensure early stability, rare-context smoothing, negligible training overhead, and zero test-time cost.

We provided complete implementation utilities and a rigorous evaluation plan on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100, including robustness suites, ablations, sensitivity analyses, and fairness safeguards. The current artifact validates feasibility and measurement instrumentation but contains no numerical results; accordingly, we refrain from efficacy claims. The next step is to execute the full suite with baselines—vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM [zheng_2024_knowledge], IPWD and weighted soft labels [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]—and GroupDRO-only, and to report statistically significant gains in worst-group accuracy and calibration with stable training and small overhead. Longer term, we will extend CCAD to detection and segmentation via region-level contexts and to multimodal KD via environmental embeddings, and combine it with feature-map and information-theoretic distillation for complementary benefits [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational], alongside broader developments in causal and multi-level distillation, dataset distillation, and anytime adaptation [author_year_comprehensive, author_year_multi, author_year_large, author_year_anyda].

@article{chung_2020_feature,
 abstract = {Feature maps contain rich information about image intensity and spatial
correlation. However, previous online knowledge distillation methods only
utilize the class probabilities. Thus in this paper, we propose an online
knowledge distillation method that transfers not only the knowledge of the
class probabilities but also that of the feature map using the adversarial
training framework. We train multiple networks simultaneously by employing
discriminators to distinguish the feature map distributions of different
networks. Each network has its corresponding discriminator which discriminates
the feature map from its own as fake while classifying that of the other
network as real. By training a network to fool the corresponding discriminator,
it can learn the other network's feature map distribution. We show that our
method performs better than the conventional direct alignment method such as L1
and is more suitable for online distillation. Also, we propose a novel cyclic
learning scheme for training more than two networks together. We have applied
our method to various network architectures on the classification task and
discovered a significant improvement of performance especially in the case of
training a pair of a small network and a large one.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.01775v3.pdf},
 author = {Inseop Chung and SeongUk Park and Jangho Kim and Nojun Kwak},
 title = {Feature-map-level Online Adversarial Knowledge Distillation},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{feng_2020_unsupervised,
 abstract = {Conventional unsupervised multi-source domain adaptation (UMDA) methods
assume all source domains can be accessed directly. This neglects the
privacy-preserving policy, that is, all the data and computations must be kept
decentralized. There exists three problems in this scenario: (1) Minimizing the
domain distance requires the pairwise calculation of the data from source and
target domains, which is not accessible. (2) The communication cost and privacy
security limit the application of UMDA methods (e.g., the domain adversarial
training). (3) Since users have no authority to check the data quality, the
irrelevant or malicious source domains are more likely to appear, which causes
negative transfer. In this study, we propose a privacy-preserving UMDA paradigm
named Knowledge Distillation based Decentralized Domain Adaptation (KD3A),
which performs domain adaptation through the knowledge distillation on models
from different source domains. KD3A solves the above problems with three
components: (1) A multi-source knowledge distillation method named Knowledge
Vote to learn high-quality domain consensus knowledge. (2) A dynamic weighting
strategy named Consensus Focus to identify both the malicious and irrelevant
domains. (3) A decentralized optimization strategy for domain distance named
BatchNorm MMD. The extensive experiments on DomainNet demonstrate that KD3A is
robust to the negative transfer and brings a 100x reduction of communication
cost compared with other decentralized UMDA methods. Moreover, our KD3A
significantly outperforms state-of-the-art UMDA approaches.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2011.09757v7.pdf},
 author = {Hao-Zhe Feng and Zhaoyang You and Minghao Chen and Tianye Zhang and Minfeng Zhu and Fei Wu and Chao Wu and Wei Chen},
 title = {KD3A: Unsupervised Multi-Source Decentralized Domain Adaptation via Knowledge Distillation},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{mazaheri_2020_robust,
 abstract = {Varying domains and biased datasets can lead to differences between the
training and the target distributions, known as covariate shift. Current
approaches for alleviating this often rely on estimating the ratio of training
and target probability density functions. These techniques require parameter
tuning and can be unstable across different datasets. We present a new method
for handling covariate shift using the empirical cumulative distribution
function estimates of the target distribution by a rigorous generalization of a
recent idea proposed by Vapnik and Izmailov. Further, we show experimentally
that our method is more robust in its predictions, is not reliant on parameter
tuning and shows similar classification performance compared to the current
state-of-the-art techniques on synthetic and real datasets.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.12687v1.pdf},
 author = {Bijan Mazaheri and Siddharth Jain and Jehoshua Bruck},
 title = {Robust Correction of Sampling Bias using Cumulative Distribution Functions},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{niu_2022_respecting,
 abstract = {Knowledge distillation (KD) is essentially a process of transferring a
teacher model's behavior, e.g., network response, to a student model. The
network response serves as additional supervision to formulate the machine
domain, which uses the data collected from the human domain as a transfer set.
Traditional KD methods hold an underlying assumption that the data collected in
both human domain and machine domain are both independent and identically
distributed (IID). We point out that this naive assumption is unrealistic and
there is indeed a transfer gap between the two domains. Although the gap offers
the student model external knowledge from the machine domain, the imbalanced
teacher knowledge would make us incorrectly estimate how much to transfer from
teacher to student per sample on the non-IID transfer set. To tackle this
challenge, we propose Inverse Probability Weighting Distillation (IPWD) that
estimates the propensity score of a training sample belonging to the machine
domain, and assigns its inverse amount to compensate for under-represented
samples. Experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet demonstrate the effectiveness of
IPWD for both two-stage distillation and one-stage self-distillation.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.12787v1.pdf},
 author = {Yulei Niu and Long Chen and Chang Zhou and Hanwang Zhang},
 github_url = {https://github.com/bellymonster/Weighted-Soft-Label-Distillation},
 title = {Respecting Transfer Gap in Knowledge Distillation},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{niu_2022_respecting,
 abstract = {Knowledge distillation (KD) is essentially a process of transferring a
teacher model's behavior, e.g., network response, to a student model. The
network response serves as additional supervision to formulate the machine
domain, which uses the data collected from the human domain as a transfer set.
Traditional KD methods hold an underlying assumption that the data collected in
both human domain and machine domain are both independent and identically
distributed (IID). We point out that this naive assumption is unrealistic and
there is indeed a transfer gap between the two domains. Although the gap offers
the student model external knowledge from the machine domain, the imbalanced
teacher knowledge would make us incorrectly estimate how much to transfer from
teacher to student per sample on the non-IID transfer set. To tackle this
challenge, we propose Inverse Probability Weighting Distillation (IPWD) that
estimates the propensity score of a training sample belonging to the machine
domain, and assigns its inverse amount to compensate for under-represented
samples. Experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet demonstrate the effectiveness of
IPWD for both two-stage distillation and one-stage self-distillation.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.12787v1.pdf},
 author = {Yulei Niu and Long Chen and Chang Zhou and Hanwang Zhang},
 github_url = {https://github.com/bellymonster/Weighted-Soft-Label-Distillation},
 title = {Respecting Transfer Gap in Knowledge Distillation},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{ojha_2022_what,
 abstract = {Knowledge distillation aims to transfer useful information from a teacher
network to a student network, with the primary goal of improving the student's
performance for the task at hand. Over the years, there has a been a deluge of
novel techniques and use cases of knowledge distillation. Yet, despite the
various improvements, there seems to be a glaring gap in the community's
fundamental understanding of the process. Specifically, what is the knowledge
that gets distilled in knowledge distillation? In other words, in what ways
does the student become similar to the teacher? Does it start to localize
objects in the same way? Does it get fooled by the same adversarial samples?
Does its data invariance properties become similar? Our work presents a
comprehensive study to try to answer these questions. We show that existing
methods can indeed indirectly distill these properties beyond improving task
performance. We further study why knowledge distillation might work this way,
and show that our findings have practical implications as well.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2205.16004v3.pdf},
 author = {Utkarsh Ojha and Yuheng Li and Anirudh Sundara Rajan and Yingyu Liang and Yong Jae Lee},
 title = {What Knowledge Gets Distilled in Knowledge Distillation?},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{yang_2022_divide,
 abstract = {Domain Adaptation of Black-box Predictors (DABP) aims to learn a model on an
unlabeled target domain supervised by a black-box predictor trained on a source
domain. It does not require access to both the source-domain data and the
predictor parameters, thus addressing the data privacy and portability issues
of standard domain adaptation. Existing DABP approaches mostly rely on model
distillation from the black-box predictor, \emph{i.e.}, training the model with
its noisy target-domain predictions, which however inevitably introduces the
confirmation bias accumulated from the prediction noises. To mitigate such
bias, we propose a new method, named BETA, to incorporate knowledge
distillation and noisy label learning into one coherent framework. This is
enabled by a new divide-to-adapt strategy. BETA divides the target domain into
an easy-to-adapt subdomain with less noise and a hard-to-adapt subdomain. Then
it deploys mutually-teaching twin networks to filter the predictor errors for
each other and improve them progressively, from the easy to hard subdomains. As
such, BETA effectively purifies the noisy labels and reduces error
accumulation. We theoretically show that the target error of BETA is minimized
by decreasing the noise ratio of the subdomains. Extensive experiments
demonstrate BETA outperforms existing methods on all DABP benchmarks, and is
even comparable with the standard domain adaptation methods that use the
source-domain data.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2205.14467v1.pdf},
 author = {Jianfei Yang and Xiangyu Peng and Kai Wang and Zheng Zhu and Jiashi Feng and Lihua Xie and Yang You},
 github_url = {https://github.com/xyupeng/BETA},
 title = {Divide to Adapt: Mitigating Confirmation Bias for Domain Adaptation of Black-Box Predictors},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{zheng_2024_knowledge,
 abstract = {As a technique to bridge logit matching and probability distribution
matching, temperature scaling plays a pivotal role in knowledge distillation
(KD). Conventionally, temperature scaling is applied to both teacher's logits
and student's logits in KD. Motivated by some recent works, in this paper, we
drop instead temperature scaling on the student side, and systematically study
the resulting variant of KD, dubbed transformed teacher matching (TTM). By
reinterpreting temperature scaling as a power transform of probability
distribution, we show that in comparison with the original KD, TTM has an
inherent R\'enyi entropy term in its objective function, which serves as an
extra regularization term. Extensive experiment results demonstrate that thanks
to this inherent regularization, TTM leads to trained students with better
generalization than the original KD. To further enhance student's capability to
match teacher's power transformed probability distribution, we introduce a
sample-adaptive weighting coefficient into TTM, yielding a novel distillation
approach dubbed weighted TTM (WTTM). It is shown, by comprehensive experiments,
that although WTTM is simple, it is effective, improves upon TTM, and achieves
state-of-the-art accuracy performance. Our source code is available at
https://github.com/zkxufo/TTM.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.11148v2.pdf},
 author = {Kaixiang Zheng and En-Hui Yang},
 github_url = {https://github.com/zkxufo/TTM},
 title = {Knowledge Distillation Based on Transformed Teacher Matching},
 year = {2024}
}

@article{zhou_2021_rethinking,
 abstract = {Knowledge distillation is an effective approach to leverage a well-trained
network or an ensemble of them, named as the teacher, to guide the training of
a student network. The outputs from the teacher network are used as soft labels
for supervising the training of a new network. Recent studies
\citep{muller2019does,yuan2020revisiting} revealed an intriguing property of
the soft labels that making labels soft serves as a good regularization to the
student network. From the perspective of statistical learning, regularization
aims to reduce the variance, however how bias and variance change is not clear
for training with soft labels. In this paper, we investigate the bias-variance
tradeoff brought by distillation with soft labels. Specifically, we observe
that during training the bias-variance tradeoff varies sample-wisely. Further,
under the same distillation temperature setting, we observe that the
distillation performance is negatively associated with the number of some
specific samples, which are named as regularization samples since these samples
lead to bias increasing and variance decreasing. Nevertheless, we empirically
find that completely filtering out regularization samples also deteriorates
distillation performance. Our discoveries inspired us to propose the novel
weighted soft labels to help the network adaptively handle the sample-wise
bias-variance tradeoff. Experiments on standard evaluation benchmarks validate
the effectiveness of our method. Our code is available at
\url{https://github.com/bellymonster/Weighted-Soft-Label-Distillation}.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2102.00650v1.pdf},
 author = {Helong Zhou and Liangchen Song and Jiajie Chen and Ye Zhou and Guoli Wang and Junsong Yuan and Qian Zhang},
 title = {Rethinking Soft Labels for Knowledge Distillation: A Bias–Variance Tradeoff Perspective},
 year = {2021}
}
