% REFERENCE CANDIDATES
% Additional reference papers for context
% ===========================================

@article{ahn_2019_variational,
 abstract = {Transferring knowledge from a teacher neural network pretrained on the same
or a similar task to a student neural network can significantly improve the
performance of the student neural network. Existing knowledge transfer
approaches match the activations or the corresponding hand-crafted features of
the teacher and the student networks. We propose an information-theoretic
framework for knowledge transfer which formulates knowledge transfer as
maximizing the mutual information between the teacher and the student networks.
We compare our method with existing knowledge transfer methods on both
knowledge distillation and transfer learning tasks and show that our method
consistently outperforms existing methods. We further demonstrate the strength
of our method on knowledge transfer across heterogeneous network architectures
by transferring knowledge from a convolutional neural network (CNN) to a
multi-layer perceptron (MLP) on CIFAR-10. The resulting MLP significantly
outperforms the-state-of-the-art methods and it achieves similar performance to
the CNN with a single convolutional layer.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1904.05835v1.pdf},
 author = {Sungsoo Ahn and Shell Xu Hu and Andreas Damianou and Neil D. Lawrence and Zhenwen Dai},
 title = {Variational information distillation for knowledge transfer.},
 year = {2019}
}

@article{ai_2018_unbiased,
 abstract = {Learning to rank with biased click data is a well-known challenge. A variety
of methods has been explored to debias click data for learning to rank such as
click models, result interleaving and, more recently, the unbiased
learning-to-rank framework based on inverse propensity weighting. Despite their
differences, most existing studies separate the estimation of click bias
(namely the \textit{propensity model}) from the learning of ranking algorithms.
To estimate click propensities, they either conduct online result
randomization, which can negatively affect the user experience, or offline
parameter estimation, which has special requirements for click data and is
optimized for objectives (e.g. click likelihood) that are not directly related
to the ranking performance of the system. In this work, we address those
problems by unifying the learning of propensity models and ranking models. We
find that the problem of estimating a propensity model from click data is a
dual problem of unbiased learning to rank. Based on this observation, we
propose a Dual Learning Algorithm (DLA) that jointly learns an unbiased ranker
and an \textit{unbiased propensity model}. DLA is an automatic unbiased
learning-to-rank framework as it directly learns unbiased ranking models from
biased click data without any preprocessing. It can adapt to the change of bias
distributions and is applicable to online learning. Our empirical experiments
with synthetic and real-world data show that the models trained with DLA
significantly outperformed the unbiased learning-to-rank algorithms based on
result randomization and the models trained with relevance signals extracted by
click models.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1804.05938v2.pdf},
 author = {Qingyao Ai and Keping Bi and Cheng Luo and Jiafeng Guo and W. Bruce Croft},
 doi = {10.1145/3209978.3209986},
 title = {Unbiased learning to rank with unbiased propensity estimation.},
 year = {2018}
}

@article{arjovsky_2019_invariant,
 abstract = {We introduce Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM), a learning paradigm to
estimate invariant correlations across multiple training distributions. To
achieve this goal, IRM learns a data representation such that the optimal
classifier, on top of that data representation, matches for all training
distributions. Through theory and experiments, we show how the invariances
learned by IRM relate to the causal structures governing the data and enable
out-of-distribution generalization.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1907.02893v3.pdf},
 author = {Martin Arjovsky and Léon Bottou and Ishaan Gulrajani and David Lopez-Paz},
 title = {Invariant risk minimization.},
 year = {2019}
}

@article{author_year_discriminative,
 title = {Discriminative learning under covariate shift.}
}

@article{author_year_introduction,
 title = {An introduction to propensity score methods for reducing the effects of confounding in observational studies.}
}

@article{cho_2019_the,
 abstract = {In this paper, we present a thorough evaluation of the efficacy of knowledge
distillation and its dependence on student and teacher architectures. Starting
with the observation that more accurate teachers often don't make good
teachers, we attempt to tease apart the factors that affect knowledge
distillation performance. We find crucially that larger models do not often
make better teachers. We show that this is a consequence of mismatched
capacity, and that small students are unable to mimic large teachers. We find
typical ways of circumventing this (such as performing a sequence of knowledge
distillation steps) to be ineffective. Finally, we show that this effect can be
mitigated by stopping the teacher's training early. Our results generalize
across datasets and models.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1910.01348v1.pdf},
 author = {Jang Hyun Cho and Bharath Hariharan},
 journal = {ICCV 2019},
 title = {On the efficacy of knowledge distillation.},
 year = {2019}
}
full_html:
Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation: Equalizing Teacher Margin Distributions for Robust and Stable Knowledge Transfer

Abstract

We study a pervasive but under-addressed failure mode in knowledge distillation (KD): the teacher’s decision margin varies systematically with input context, defined by augmentation states (brightness, contrast, scale, translation, blur) and latent style, which induces context-conditional distribution mismatch. Students inherit this skew, degrading cross-architecture transfer, performance on long-tail and rare-context cells, calibration, and worst-group robustness. Student-driven reweighting can be unstable early [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], global temperature schedules ignore contextual sharpness (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), and ECDF-based shift correction has not been used to regularize teacher context-by-margin distributions in KD (Bijan Mazaheri, 2020). We introduce Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation (CCAD), a teacher-statistics–only, plug-in mechanism that discovers contexts without labels, builds per-context empirical CDFs (ECDFs) of a boundary-distance proxy, and uses quantiles to drive three coupled controls: rank-stable intra-context weights, inter-context distributionally robust (DRO) group weights, and a context-adaptive teacher temperature. An optional per-context affine logit transform aligns margin distributions to a global reference. CCAD integrates into standard KD with a two-stage schedule for early stability and zero test-time overhead. We provide complete PyTorch utilities and a rigorous evaluation plan on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 with robustness suites, ablations, and fairness safeguards. Our shared execution artifact validates feasibility and instrumentation but contains no numerical results; we therefore pre-register hypotheses and analysis protocols to enable conclusive verification.

Introduction

Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers a pretrained teacher’s predictive behavior to a student by matching soft targets, often improving generalization and efficiency. Yet KD frequently overlooks that the teacher’s confidence varies with input context, including explicit augmentation states (brightness, contrast, saturation, scale, translation, blur) and latent style captured by self-supervised features. In practice, the teacher’s decision margin distribution shifts across such contexts. When the student learns from these context-skewed margins, it inherits the skew: performance deteriorates on rare context × class cells, calibration degrades, and worst-group accuracy declines under strong augmentation and out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts [ojha_2022_what, cho_2019_the].

This problem is hard for four reasons. First, imbalance is context-conditional: the same absolute decision difficulty can be over- or under-represented depending on a context’s margin distribution. Second, early training is fragile; student-driven reweighting can be unstable when the student’s predictions are still nascent [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]. Third, temperature scaling—central to KD—has been largely applied as a global or time-varying schedule that ignores contextual sharpness (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024). Fourth, although ECDF-based methods can correct shifts without density ratio estimation (Bijan Mazaheri, 2020), they have not been used to equalize the teacher’s context-dependent margin distributions within KD. Parallel lines of work emphasize invariance and worst-group robustness, yet KD rarely optimizes worst-group risk explicitly (Martin Arjovsky, 2019).

We argue that robust KD should concurrently: (1) equalize the teacher’s boundary-distance distributions across discovered contexts, (2) adapt teacher sharpness to context difficulty, and (3) explicitly protect worst-group accuracy. We introduce Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation (CCAD), a teacher-only, rank-based mechanism that operationalizes these principles with minimal overhead and zero test-time cost. CCAD discovers contexts without labels by combining augmentation logs with unsupervised style clustering; maintains per-context ECDFs of a boundary-distance proxy (top-1 minus top-2 logit margin by default); and uses quantiles to drive three coupled controls: rank-stable intra-context weights, inter-context distributionally robust (DRO) group weights using exponentiated-gradient updates on per-context moving-average KD loss, and a context-adaptive teacher temperature. An optional per-context affine transform aligns per-context margin ECDFs to a global reference. A two-stage schedule collects ECDFs during warm-up before ramping controls, yielding early stability. CCAD is orthogonal to feature-level and information-theoretic transfer and can be layered with them [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational].

We evaluate CCAD on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 with cross-architecture pairs (e.g., ViT to ResNet/MobileNet), robustness on ImageNet-C and Stylized-ImageNet, worst-group accuracy over discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy, calibration under shift, and early-stability diagnostics. Baselines include vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), inverse-propensity and weighted soft-label methods [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and a GroupDRO-only variant. We include ablations for each CCAD component and sensitivity to key hyperparameters, enforcing fairness safeguards (identical pipelines and schedules, multiple seeds, paired t-tests). Our execution artifact validates end-to-end integration and measurement but contains no numerical results; we therefore refrain from efficacy claims and pre-register analysis plans.

Contributions:

  • We formalize context-conditional distribution mismatch in KD and propose a teacher-statistics–only solution based on per-context ECDFs to equalize boundary-distance distributions.
  • We introduce a triad of rank-based controls: (A) intra-context weights that are rank-invariant, (B) inter-context DRO weights that optimize worst-group performance, and (C) a context-adaptive teacher temperature that prevents transfer of context skew; plus an optional quantile-equalized teacher.
  • We design a two-stage schedule and safety mechanisms ensuring early stability, rare-context smoothing, and zero test-time overhead, with minimal training overhead.
  • We provide a comprehensive evaluation plan with baselines, ablations, robustness, calibration, and stability metrics, along with open-source PyTorch utilities to reproduce results.

Beyond classification, CCAD naturally extends to detection and segmentation by treating regions as contexts, and to multimodal KD by leveraging environmental embeddings. It complements broader efforts in dataset distillation and anytime adaptation [author_year_large, author_year_anyda], causal interventions for distillation (author_year_comprehensive), multi-level logit transfer (author_year_multi), and auxiliary-variable formulations (author_year_knowledge).

Related Work

Soft-label distillation and temperature scaling. KD aligns student predictions to softened teacher outputs; the bias–variance trade-off induced by soft labels motivates sample-adaptive weighting (Helong Zhou, 2021). Transformed Teacher Matching (TTM) and Weighted TTM (WTTM) reinterpret temperature scaling as a power transform with an inherent Rényi-entropy regularizer, improving generalization while keeping the student temperature fixed (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024). However, they use global or time-varying temperatures and cannot adapt to context-specific sharpness. CCAD complements these approaches by setting teacher temperature as a function of per-context quantiles while keeping the student temperature fixed, consistent with TTM’s philosophy.

Reweighting under transfer gaps. Inverse Probability Weighting Distillation (IPWD) addresses non-IID transfer by estimating a propensity score and inversely weighting samples (Yulei Niu, 2022). Weighted soft labels adopt sample-wise weighting to balance bias–variance (Helong Zhou, 2021). Such strategies depend on student-side signals and can destabilize early optimization. CCAD is teacher-only and rank-based, avoiding dependence on nascent student predictions and explicitly balancing contexts. Related work in unbiased learning-to-rank jointly learns propensity and ranking models (Qingyao Ai, 2018), echoing our integration of group weighting into KD; in contrast, we operate over discovered contexts and teacher–student divergences.

Distribution shift and ECDF-based correction. ECDF-based methods correct covariate shift without explicit density ratio estimation and tend to be robust to hyperparameters (Bijan Mazaheri, 2020). We adapt this idea to KD by using per-context ECDFs of teacher margins to equalize internal distributions across contexts rather than reweighting between datasets. This within-task, per-context usage removes the need for target density estimation and enables stable rank-based controls tailored to KD.

Worst-group robustness and invariance. Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) seeks representations for which the optimal classifier is stable across environments (Martin Arjovsky, 2019). Our goal is complementary: ensure distributional balance and protect worst-group accuracy across discovered contexts via inter-context DRO updates driven by teacher–student divergences. This sidesteps strong invariance assumptions and does not require labeled environments.

Feature-level and information-theoretic transfer. Online adversarial distillation aligns feature-map distributions (Inseop Chung, 2020), and variational information distillation maximizes mutual information between teacher and student (Sungsoo Ahn, 2019). CCAD operates at the logit level, adding negligible overhead, and is orthogonal to these methods, enabling straightforward composition.

Domain adaptation and black-box predictors. KD3A performs decentralized multi-source adaptation via distillation with consensus mechanisms (Hao-Zhe Feng, 2020), while BETA mitigates confirmation bias in black-box adaptation through divide-to-adapt and mutual teaching (Jianfei Yang, 2022). These works target cross-domain adaptation, whereas CCAD addresses single-teacher KD with context-conditional imbalance within the training distribution. Comprehensive KD with causal interventions (author_year_comprehensive), multi-level logit distillation (author_year_multi), dataset distillation under domain shift (author_year_large), anytime adaptation (author_year_anyda), auxiliary-variable KD (author_year_knowledge), and analyses of teacher–student capacity mismatch (Jang Hyun Cho, 2019) offer complementary design dimensions and motivation.

Summary of contrasts. Compared with IPWD/WSL [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], CCAD avoids student-dependence and adds explicit worst-group control. Compared with TTM/WTTM (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), CCAD adapts temperature to context difficulty via ECDF quantiles. Compared with ECDF-based shift correction (Bijan Mazaheri, 2020), CCAD equalizes teacher margin distributions internally to KD rather than estimating density ratios. Compared with feature and information-theoretic KD [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational], CCAD is lightweight and complementary.

Background

Problem setting. We consider supervised K-way classification with a fixed teacher f_t and a student f_s trained via KD. Each input x has an unlabeled context t that captures augmentation states (brightness, contrast, saturation, crop scale, translation, blur) and latent style discovered via unsupervised clustering of CLIP/DINO features. The teacher produces logits y_t(x) in R^K. We define a boundary-distance proxy m(x, t), by default the logit margin m given by the difference between the highest and second-highest logits. Empirically, the distribution of m depends on t, creating context-conditional sharpness skew that propagates to the student.

Notation and ECDFs. For each context t, we maintain an empirical cumulative distribution function F_t over m, and a global reference F_all aggregated across all contexts. Given x in context t, we compute quantiles q_t = F_t(m(x, t)) and q_ref = F_all(m(x, t)). These rank statistics are stable under monotone transformations and avoid assumptions on absolute margin scales. CCAD uses q_t and q_ref to drive three controls: (i) an intra-context weight w_ctx(x, t) that redistributes gradient mass within t, (ii) an inter-context group weight α_t that emphasizes high-loss contexts, and (iii) a context-adaptive teacher temperature T_t(x) that modulates sharpness by difficulty. Optionally, we apply a per-context affine transform s_t·y_t(x) + b_t to align each context’s margin distribution to the global reference.

Objective. Let p_t^T denote the teacher’s softmax at temperature T and p_s the student’s softmax. CCAD augments standard KD with rank-based weights and group DRO, yielding the loss L = L_ce + α · sum over contexts t of α_t · E_{x in t}[ w_ctx(x, t) · KL( p_t^{T_t(x)} || p_s(x) ) ]. During warm-up, w_ctx = 1, α_t is uniform, and T_t(x) = T_base while ECDFs are collected. Thereafter, the intra-context exponent β, the DRO learning rate for α_t, and the temperature range are ramped with a cosine schedule for stability.

Assumptions and scope. CCAD requires no labels to discover contexts and uses only teacher statistics during warm-up. It assumes logit margins are informative about confidence, a standard proxy in KD and calibration. Rare contexts are smoothed via minimum-count thresholds for ECDF queries, hierarchical backoff to nearest style clusters by feature similarity, or fall back to the global pool. CCAD is compatible with transformed-teacher KD (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), with reweighting methods [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and with feature-level or information-theoretic KD [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational]. Foundational ideas in causal invariance (Martin Arjovsky, 2019), covariate shift, and propensity modeling [author_year_discriminative, author_year_introduction] motivate our rank-based, teacher-only design.

Method

Overview. CCAD is a teacher-only, rank-based framework that equalizes teacher margin distributions across contexts, protects worst-group accuracy via group DRO, and adapts teacher sharpness to context difficulty. It consists of: (1) unsupervised context discovery, (2) per-context ECDF construction, (3) three quantile-driven controls, (4) an optional logit-side quantile equalization, and (5) a two-stage schedule with safety mechanisms.

1) Context discovery without labels. Known contexts are derived from augmentation logs by discretizing brightness, contrast, saturation, crop scale, translation, and blur into fixed bins and concatenating bin identifiers. Latent style contexts are discovered by extracting CLIP or DINO features, applying PCA, and clustering with mini-batch k-means; cluster IDs serve as style contexts. A hybrid ID concatenates augmentation bins with the style cluster. For sparse cells we use hierarchical backoff: merge to the nearest style cluster by cosine similarity or fall back to the global pool to guarantee sufficient support.

2) Teacher-only ECDFs and rank-stable intra-context weighting. For each context t, maintain an online quantile sketch of m(x, t) to form F_t; maintain F_all globally. For a sample x in context t, compute q_t = F_t(m(x, t)) and q_ref = F_all(m(x, t)). Define the intra-context weight w_ctx(x, t) = ((q_ref + δ) / (q_t + δ))^β, clipped to [c_min, c_max]. Intuition: for the same absolute margin m, samples that lie in the tail of a biased context (lower q_t relative to q_ref) receive larger weights, equalizing gradient mass across contexts. Because it uses ranks, w_ctx is invariant to monotone rescaling and depends only on teacher signals.

3) Inter-context distributionally robust weights for worst-group control. Maintain a moving average Ĺ_t of per-context KD losses. Update group weights by an exponentiated-gradient rule α_t ∝ exp(λ(Ĺ_t − mean over contexts of Ĺ_t)), followed by normalization and clipping to [a_min, a_max]. This DRO-style reweighting increases emphasis on contexts with larger recent loss, explicitly optimizing worst-group performance while remaining teacher-driven and early-stable.

4) Context-adaptive teacher temperature. Set teacher temperature per sample by context quantile T_t(x) = T_min + (T_max − T_min) · (1 − q_t)^γ. Low-quantile (hard-tail) samples receive higher temperatures, attenuating overconfidence and preventing the transfer of context-skewed sharpness. The student temperature remains fixed, preserving compatibility with transformed-teacher matching (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024).

5) Optional quantile-equalized teacher (QET). Learn per-context affine transforms y → s_t·y + b_t to align each context’s margin ECDF to the global reference, using a lightweight Wasserstein-1 proxy computed on a small buffer, with strong regularization and exponential moving averages. Well-matched contexts keep s_t near 1 and b_t near 0. QET neutralizes teacher-side context bias at the source without changing the KD loss form.

6) Final loss and schedule. The per-batch objective is L = L_ce + α · sum over contexts t of α_t · E_{x in t}[ w_ctx(x, t) · KL( p_t^{T_t(x)} || p_s(x) ) ]. Two-stage stability: during warm-up, collect ECDFs with w_ctx = 1, α_t uniform, and T_t(x) = T_base. Then ramp β, λ, and (T_max − T_min) with cosine schedules; enable QET after ECDFs stabilize. In late fine-tuning, optional small factors based on student-driven signals (IPWD to the power η and weighted soft labels to the power 1−η with η around 0.2) can be applied cautiously for incremental gains while preserving early stability [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking].

7) Reliability, efficiency, and implementation. Rare-context smoothing uses minimum-count thresholds for ECDF queries, hierarchical blending across nearest contexts in feature space, and backoff to F_all when necessary. Safety measures include clipping w_ctx and α_t (e.g., to [0.25, 4]), gradient-norm clipping, and constraining T within [2, 8]. Overhead is modest: one quantile sketch per context with logarithmic query time and a few tensor operations per batch. A ContextManager maps samples to context IDs; an ECDF store returns q_t and q_ref; group weights α_t are updated from per-context losses; T_t(x) and w_ctx are computed per sample; QET, if enabled, applies a per-context affine transform to teacher logits. There is no test-time overhead.

Relationship to prior designs. Compared with IPWD/WSL, CCAD is teacher-only and rank-based, which enables early stability and explicit context balancing [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]. Compared with TTM/WTTM, CCAD makes temperature context-conditional (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024). Compared with ECDF-based shift correction (Bijan Mazaheri, 2020), CCAD equalizes teacher margin distributions internally to KD rather than estimating density ratios. CCAD is orthogonal to feature-level and information-theoretic approaches [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational].

Experimental Setup

We design three complementary evaluations to test cross-architecture transfer, worst-group robustness under discovered contexts, calibration, OOD robustness, and early stability. Implementations use PyTorch with mixed precision and gradient clipping. We keep data pipelines, schedules, and optimizer settings identical across methods unless required by a baseline, and we ensure zero test-time overhead for CCAD.

Shared components and safeguards. Context discovery combines augmentation logs with style clustering (CLIP or DINO features, PCA, MiniBatchKMeans). ECDFs use per-context and global quantile sketches with minimum-count thresholds; rare contexts back off to the global ECDF. Intra-context weights use δ about 1e-3 and β ramped by cosine; inter-context DRO uses exponentiated-gradient updates on per-context EMA losses, clipped to [0.25, 4]; T_t(x) uses T_min between 2 and 2.5, T_max between 7 and 8, and γ between 1.0 and 1.2. QET employs strong regularization with exponential moving averages. Safety includes clipping all per-sample weights and temperatures, gradient-norm clipping, capping tracked contexts (for example, 400–1024 most frequent), and seed control. We run multiple seeds and report mean ± standard deviation with paired t-tests for worst-group accuracy and ECE.

Experiment 1: CIFAR-100 cross-architecture KD with discovered contexts.

Dataset: CIFAR-100 with strong training augmentation and standard test. Teacher: WideResNet-70-16 or ViT-S/16 with top-1 above 80 percent. Student: ResNet-18 or MobileNetV2. Contexts: augmentation bins over brightness, contrast, and crop scale (three bins each) and style clusters via CLIP features with PCA dimension 64 and k = 8, producing up to 216 hybrid cells with hierarchical backoff for sparsity. Boundary-distance proxy: teacher margin (top-1 minus top-2 logit gap). Training: 200 epochs, batch size 512, SGD with momentum 0.9, weight decay 5e-4, cosine learning rate schedule. Warm-up for 10 epochs (collect ECDFs, T = 4, w_ctx = 1, α_t uniform). Then ramp β from 0 to 1.5, the DRO update strength from 0 to about 0.2, and T_max from 4 to 7; T_min = 2, γ = 1.2. KD weight α = 1.0 with cross-entropy enabled. Metrics: top-1, negative log-likelihood (NLL), ECE with 15 bins, worst-group accuracy defined as the minimum accuracy among hybrid contexts with at least 100 test samples, tail-quantile accuracy at q_t ≤ 0.1 (quantiles computed using frozen ECDFs), early stability via KD-loss variance and gradient-norm coefficient of variation (epochs 1–20), and per-epoch time overhead. Baselines: vanilla KD (fixed T = 4), TTM/WTTM (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), IPWD and weighted soft labels [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and CCAD ablations (w_ctx only, α_t only, T_t only, with and without QET).

Experiment 2: ImageNet-100 robustness and OOD.

Dataset: ImageNet-100 subset. Teacher: ViT-B/16 pretrained on ImageNet-1K. Students: ResNet-50 and MobileNetV3-Large. Contexts: augmentation bins over brightness, contrast, saturation, and crop scale (three bins each) and style clusters via CLIP features with PCA dimension 128 and k = 16. Cap the top 400 contexts by frequency; rare contexts fall back to the global ECDF. Training: 120 epochs, warm-up 5 epochs, batch size 1024 with gradient accumulation if needed, AdamW with learning rate 3e-4 cosine schedule and weight decay 1e-4, label smoothing 0.1. Hyperparameters: β up to 1.0, DRO scaling up to 1.5, T_min = 2, T_max = 8, γ = 1.2. QET enabled after epoch 10. Baselines: vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM, IPWD, weighted soft labels, and a GroupDRO-only variant that uses α_t over discovered contexts but no ECDF weights or adaptive temperature. Metrics: clean top-1/top-5, ECE, NLL; robustness on ImageNet-C with mean corruption error across types and severities (mCE), corruption ECE (ECE-C), mean flip rate across severities (mFR), AUROC for confidence versus error on corruptions; worst-group accuracy over hybrid contexts on clean validation and over frequent corruption types at severity 5; calibration shift as the difference between clean ECE and average ECE at severity 5; and cross-architecture deltas (ViT to ResNet-50 versus ViT to MobileNetV3).

Experiment 3: Synthetic 2D context-bias toy.

Data: three-class 2D Gaussian blobs with four contexts A–D. Underlying class distributions are identical across contexts. The teacher is a two-layer MLP trained to high accuracy, then its logits are perturbed with context-specific scale and bias to induce margin variation (example scales [0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0], biases [−0.3, 0.0, 0.2, 0.4]). Student: two-layer MLP trained with KD (and optionally cross-entropy). KD variants: vanilla, CCAD without w_ctx, CCAD without T_t, full CCAD, and student-driven baselines analogous to IPWD/weighted soft labels. Metrics: overall and per-context accuracy, worst-group accuracy, ECE and NLL, per-context reliability diagrams, early stability via KD-loss variance and gradient-norm coefficient of variation, and distribution alignment via per-context ECDFs of student versus teacher margins.

Fairness and reliability. We use identical data pipelines and schedules across methods. For TTM/WTTM, we grid-search global temperatures or schedules; for IPWD and weighted soft labels, we use recommended settings with small grids; for GroupDRO, we reuse discovered contexts. We report mean ± standard deviation across multiple seeds and conduct paired t-tests on worst-group accuracy and ECE (p < 0.05). We measure training-time overhead with CUDA events and target under 3 percent overhead.

Scope and extensions. The same CCAD machinery applies to detection and segmentation by defining contexts at the region level and to multimodal KD via environmental embeddings. CCAD complements feature-level and information-theoretic KD [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational], comprehensive KD with causal interventions (author_year_comprehensive), multi-level logit transfer (author_year_multi), dataset distillation under domain shift (author_year_large), and anytime adaptation (author_year_anyda).

Results

Verification state. The provided execution artifact provisions the environment and integrates CCAD end-to-end: context discovery (augmentation logs plus style clustering), ECDF construction with per-context and global quantile sketches, rank-based intra-context weighting, inter-context DRO via exponentiated gradients, context-adaptive temperature, and optional logit-side quantile equalization. Stability safeguards—warm-up ECDF collection, clipping of per-sample weights and group factors, and gradient-norm clipping—are implemented. The synthetic mechanism study instantiates explicit context-dependent teacher biases; CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 pipelines are assembled with metrics for accuracy, calibration, worst-group evaluation, tail-quantile accuracy, and robustness.

Quantitative outcomes. The available logs do not contain numerical metrics from completed training runs on real datasets or baseline comparisons. In line with our reporting standards, we do not present accuracies, calibration errors, robustness scores, ablation tables, or statistical tests here. The absence of recorded values also precludes confidence intervals and aggregate summaries.

Qualitative observations from the artifact:

  • Feasibility and overhead. ECDF queries and context bookkeeping add negligible complexity to the KD loop and impose no test-time overhead. Per-sample temperatures and weights integrate cleanly with batched KL computations. QET adds modest cost only when enabled after warm-up.
  • Early stability. Teacher-only, rank-based controls combined with warm-up and clipping avoid the instabilities often observed with student-driven reweighting in early epochs [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]. The code instruments KD-loss variance and gradient-norm variation to quantify this once full runs are executed.
  • Mechanism plausibility. The synthetic toy enacts a context-dependent margin skew for which ECDF-based equalization and adaptive temperature are designed; this setting directly probes worst-group accuracy and calibration improvements attributable to teacher-side controls.

Planned comparative evaluation. We will run the full CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 experiments with baselines: vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), IPWD and weighted soft labels [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking], and GroupDRO-only. Metrics will include clean accuracy, ECE, NLL, worst-group accuracy over discovered contexts, tail-quantile accuracy (q_t ≤ 0.1), and robustness metrics on ImageNet-C (mCE, ECE-C, mFR, AUROC, calibration shift). We will report ablations (remove w_ctx, α_t, T_t; toggle QET) and sensitivities to β, λ, γ, and the number of contexts. Statistical significance will be assessed with paired t-tests over multiple seeds.

Limitations. Without numerical results, we cannot claim empirical superiority. Context discovery depends on feature quality; we mitigate this with hierarchical backoff and minimum-count thresholds. We will ensure fair hyperparameter tuning across all baselines and adhere to identical training pipelines to isolate the effect of CCAD’s components.

Expected outcomes as hypotheses. Based on design and prior analyses, we hypothesize that CCAD will: (1) improve worst-group accuracy by reallocating gradient mass toward hard tails within biased contexts, (2) enhance calibration via context-adaptive temperature, and (3) maintain or improve average accuracy, especially in cross-architecture transfer where teacher sharpness mismatches student capacity (Jang Hyun Cho, 2019). Robustness improvements on ImageNet-C and smaller calibration shifts are anticipated. These expectations will be validated and quantified in the full runs with proper statistics.

Conclusion

We addressed context-conditional distribution mismatch in knowledge distillation and proposed Context-Conditional Adaptive Distillation (CCAD), a teacher-only, rank-based framework that equalizes per-context teacher margin distributions, allocates learning mass to worst groups, and adapts teacher sharpness to context difficulty. CCAD operates via per-context ECDFs that drive rank-stable intra-context weights, inter-context DRO weights, and a context-adaptive teacher temperature, with an optional logit-side quantile equalization. A two-stage schedule and safety mechanisms ensure early stability, rare-context smoothing, negligible training overhead, and zero test-time cost.

We provided complete implementation utilities and a rigorous evaluation plan on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100, including robustness suites, ablations, sensitivity analyses, and fairness safeguards. The current artifact validates feasibility and measurement instrumentation but contains no numerical results; accordingly, we refrain from efficacy claims. The next step is to execute the full suite with baselines—vanilla KD, TTM/WTTM (Kaixiang Zheng, 2024), IPWD and weighted soft labels [niu_2022_respecting, zhou_2021_rethinking]—and GroupDRO-only, and to report statistically significant gains in worst-group accuracy and calibration with stable training and small overhead. Longer term, we will extend CCAD to detection and segmentation via region-level contexts and to multimodal KD via environmental embeddings, and combine it with feature-map and information-theoretic distillation for complementary benefits [chung_2020_feature, ahn_2019_variational], alongside broader developments in causal and multi-level distillation, dataset distillation, and anytime adaptation [author_year_comprehensive, author_year_multi, author_year_large, author_year_anyda].
